attributes.stem: A 70-year-old male is in hospital one-day post total hip replacement. His initial analgesia was 5-10mg immediate-release oxycodone 4 hourly PRN, however his pain has been well-controlled with 4 doses of 5 mg since theatre. He has not previously taken opioid medications. However, his morning blood test one day post-op show a deteriorating renal function (GFR 25, down from 55 on admission bloods). The treating team consider the need to switch his oral oxycodone to another opioid.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following opioid medication and doses should the patient be commenced on?
options: Subcutaneous fentanyl injection 40-75 microg 4 hourly PRN
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting with an acute deterioration in renal function, necessitating a change of opioid analgesia. Oxycodone is the preferred oral opioid in the instance of mild-moderate renal impairment. However, for severe renal impairment (e.g. eGFR <30), buprenorphine or fentanyl are the preferred agents.

Subcutaneous fentanyl injection 40-75 microg 4 hourly PRN would be the safest opioid for this patient given his deteriorating renal function, and the dose is roughly equi-analgesic to the patient's current dose of oxycodone.

Oral morphine is rarely used in the management of acute pain, and is more commonly used in palliative care settings. Moreover, morphine's active metabolites are renally excreted, and may accumulate and result in respiratory depression if there is renal dysfunction. As such, morphine would not be the most appropriate analgesic for this patient. Finally, the equianalgesic ratio of oral oxycodone to oral morphine is 1:1.5, and thus in this patient the equivalent dose of oral morphine would be 7.5 - 15mg q4hr PRN, not 15 - 20 mg q4hr PRN.

Tramadol should be avoided with renal impairment, as its mu-opioid effect relies on the conversion to active metabolites, which can accumulate in the instance of renal impairment, and increase the risk of opioid-induced respiratory depression. Tramadol should be avoided in this patient due to his deteriorating renal function. Moreover, the equivalent dose of tramadol would be 37.5 - 75mg q4hr, not 100 - 150mg.

Fentanyl patches should be avoided in opioid-naive patients, and may take up to 24 hours to have an analgesic effect. Fentanyl patches are contraindicated for the management of acute and post-operative pain because of the high risk of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment.

Oxycodone does have renally excreted active metabolites, but their effects are normally clinically insignificant, even in patients with mild to moderate renal impairment. However, if impairment is severe, concern about accumulation means that an alternative opioid that has no renally excreted active metabolites is preferable. Continuing oxycodone at the same dose is not the most appropriate analgesia and there are safer alternatives, such as subcutaneous fentanyl, thus it is not correct.

When changing opioid medications, it is important to consider how much of the first opioid the patient has been using, as well as calculating the equianalgesic doses of the new opioid. It is important to calculate the opioid dose equivalent when changing to another opioid medication to ensure adequate analgesia is maintained while minimising the risk of overdose and side effects. The Oral Morphine Equivalent Daily Dose (oMEDD) is commonly used for these calculations, whereby a conversion factor estimates the equivalent dose of morphine. While sources may vary, the opioid conversion ratio between oral morphine and tramadol is roughly 1:0.2, 1:1.5 for oxycodone, 1:3 for transdermal fentanyl, 1:5 for hydromorphone and 1:40 for buprenorphine.

Sublingual buprenorphine would also be appropriate and safe for use in this patient, however its use and availability varies significantly among different Australian states. The risk of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment is the same as conventional opioids in the acute pain setting. However, compared to conventional opioids, there is a slower time to peak effect if PRN dose intervals of buprenorphine are ordered, and thus there is a risk of "dose stacking".
learningPoints: When changing a patient from one opioid to another, take into account equianalgesic doses and how much opioid the patient has already required
In a patient with severe renal impairment, use of an opioid that does not have renally excreted active metabolites is preferred
references: Faculty of Pain Medication (ANZCA). Opioid Calculator. Accessed 11 June, 2024. http://www.opioidcalculator.com.au

Schug SA, Palmer GM, Scott DA, Alcock M, Halliwell R, Mott JF, APM:SE Working Group of the Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists and Faculty of Pain Medicine. Acute Pain Management: Scientific Evidence (5th edition). ANZCA & FPM, Melbourne, 2020. Accessed 11 June, 2024. https://www.anzca.edu.au/resources/college-publications/acute-pain-management/apmse5.pdf

Roy PJ, Weltman M, Dember LM, Liebschutz J, Jhamb M; HOPE Consortium. Pain management in patients with chronic kidney disease and end-stage kidney disease. Curr Opin Nephrol Hypertens. 2020 Nov;29(6):671-680. doi: 10.1097/MNH.0000000000000646.
Faculty of Pain Medicine (ANZCA), 2019. 'Opioid Dose Equivalence', accessed 12/4/20, https://www.anzca.edu.au/

Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists (ANZCA) and Faculty of Pain Medicine. PS41 (G) position statement on acute pain management 2023. Available from: https://www.anzca.edu.au/getattachment/558316c5-ea93-457c-b51f-d57556b0ffa7/PS41(G)-Position-statement-on-acute-pain-management-2023#page=
